name: Alexander B. Chen
subtitle: Shady Side Academy · Class of 2027
intro: I'm a high school junior in Pittsburgh. I think about why things are true for a living — usually at 5:15 a.m. before swim practice, sometimes during it, and occasionally when I should be sleeping. I write proofs, write textbooks nobody asked for, and run a math olympiad I founded because the existing ones weren't enough.
tagline: Chasing the why behind the universe's hidden, counterintuitive rules — one proof, one lap, and one card trick at a time.

homeBlurbs.label: What I do
homeBlurbs.body: Mostly mathematics. Some research (random Schrödinger operators with Prof. Liu at Texas A&M). A 360-page multivariable calculus textbook I wrote because my teacher dared me to. An olympiad I founded because I thought my school needed one.

homeBlurbs.label: What I'm not doing math
homeBlurbs.body: Swimming 16+ hours a week, playing piano badly enough to keep it humble, skiing every break, or doing self-working card tricks that are secretly modular arithmetic.

about: I'm a high school junior at Shady Side Academy in Pittsburgh. I treat mathematics like a language I'm trying to speak fluently — not a test I'm trying to ace. Most of what I care about lives in the gap between knowing how something works and understanding why it must.

Outside the classroom you'll find me swimming 16+ hours a week, writing problems for the math olympiad I founded, working on a research project in random Schrödinger operators, or quietly losing at piano sight-reading.

research.title: Research
research.body: Working with Prof. Wencai Liu at Texas A&M on eigenvector behavior in discrete Random Schrödinger Operators. We study the Anderson model — a tridiagonal matrix with i.i.d. Bernoulli diagonal entries — and look for localization: exponential decay of eigenvectors caused by disorder in the potential.
research.note: My current task is building a numerically stable computational framework to observe decay behavior at scale without it getting drowned in rounding error.

projects.title: A 360-Page Multivariable Calculus Textbook
projects.body: What started as a joke from my teacher — "if you hate this textbook so much, write your own" — turned into a year-long obsession with clarity. I wrote, illustrated (in iPe + LaTeX), and peer-reviewed monthly editions of a 360-page text that's now a reference for this year's class.

projects.title: SSAMO — Shady Side Academy Math Olympiad
projects.body: The first annual math olympiad in our school's history. I pitched it to the school board, recruited problem writers, proctors, and graders from the math team, and designed problems that reward sustained reasoning over quick tricks.

projects.title: Mock AMC on AoPS
projects.body: A friend and I compiled our "shortlist" of brainstormed problems into a full mock AMC and posted it on the Art of Problem Solving forums. We accidentally predicted a real AMC question about Markov chains and jumping frogs.

projects.title: Self-Working Card Magic
projects.body: A COVID-era rabbit hole that became a teaching tool. I invent tricks rooted in modular arithmetic and combinatorial invariants — including a routine that's really just linear congruences mod 52 — and run them for kids at my local Chinese school.

recommendations.title: Evan Chen — EGMO
recommendations.note: If you want to actually learn olympiad geometry instead of memorizing lemmas, start here. The chapter on the incenter/excenter lemma alone changed how I see triangles.

recommendations.title: Aditya Khurmi — MONT
recommendations.note: The number theory book I wish I'd had earlier. LTE chapter is worth the price by itself — taught me how to not fake-solve.

recommendations.title: 3Blue1Brown — Essence of Linear Algebra
recommendations.note: The visual intuition I tried to pour into my multivariable textbook started here.

activities.title: Swimming
activities.body: ~10 years competitive, 16+ hours/week. 3× WPIAL qualifier, State relay participant (6th in state). This year: chasing an individual State qualification. The 5:15 a.m. alarm has aged me a decade.

activities.title: Piano
activities.body: Several hours a week on the bench. The one place I don't have to be "correct" or "fast" — just make the structure sound beautiful.

activities.title: Skiing
activities.body: Every winter and spring break: Vail, Breckenridge, Keystone, Whistler. A sensory counterweight to proof-work and pool laps. I will be on the slopes the same week I take USAMO and I will not apologize.

activities.title: DECA
activities.body: Where I learned to speak a bulletproof argument out loud — the same confidence I used to pitch SSAMO to the school board.

sessions.title: Problem Sessions
sessions.subtitle: Based off the office hours system at the math camp that didn't take me. I need to talk about math with someone or I'll keep dreaming about cyclic quadrilaterals. Apply below — I'll get back to you when I'm not in the pool, in class, or asleep before 9:30 p.m.
rules: 30 minutes on Zoom. I'll bring problems. You bring a notebook.
Any level from AMC-curious to olympiad-grade. I'll match the difficulty (or admit I can't).
I respond in batches on weekends. If you don't hear back in a week, I'm probably at a swim meet.
Cost: free. Bring me one (1) interesting problem in return.
levels: Still figuring out what an integral is
sessions.successHeading: Got it — you're in the queue.
sessions.successBody: I'll get back to you within a week. If you don't hear from me, assume I drowned at a meet and try again.

contact.blurb: Always happy to talk math, swap problems, or trade card-trick secrets.
